subject: ap-government
title: Quantitative Analysis — Congressional Hearing Days Investigating Executive Branch, 1969–2014
stimulus: Source: Data from Kriner and Schickler, 2016. Bar graph showing Congressional Hearing Days in the House that investigated the Executive Branch from 1969 to 2014, coded by whether the House was controlled by the president's party or the opposing party.

parts.prompt: Using the bar graph, identify the president who faced the fewest number of investigative hearing days in a single year when the House was controlled by the opposing party.

scoring_points.description: Using the bar graph, identify the president who faced the fewest number of investigative hearing days in a single year when the House was controlled by the opposing party.

required_elements: Identifies Clinton as the president with fewest investigative hearing days when the opposing party controlled the House
alternatives.correct_example: Clinton faced the fewest investigative hearing days in a single year when the House was controlled by the opposing party.

wrong_examples: Nixon — Nixon had significantly more hearing days during the Watergate era when the opposing party controlled the House.
Obama — Obama's administration also faced more hearing days than Clinton's when the House was controlled by Republicans.
common_traps: Look specifically at years when the opposing party controlled the House — not all years shown in the graph
Only Clinton earns the point based on the data
scoring_points.official_rubric: One point is earned for identifying the president who faced the fewest number of investigative hearing days in a single year when the House was controlled by the opposing party. Acceptable response: Clinton.

sample_responses.response: Clinton faced the fewest investigative hearing days in a single year when the opposing party controlled the House.
sample_responses.commentary: Correctly identifies Clinton based on the bar graph data. Earns the point.

sample_responses.response: Nixon faced the fewest investigative hearing days when the opposing party controlled the House.
sample_responses.commentary: Incorrect — Nixon faced significantly more hearing days during the Watergate era. Only Clinton earns the point. 0 pts.

parts.prompt: Describe the relationship between investigative hearing days and party control of the House and the presidency as shown in the bar graph.
parts.scoring_notes: Accept any accurate description of the relationship based on the data — more hearing days when opposing party controls the House, fewer when same party controls.

scoring_points.description: Describe the relationship between investigative hearing days and party control of the House and the presidency as shown in the bar graph.

required_elements: Accurately describes that more hearing days occur when the opposing party controls the House
alternatives.correct_example: The data shows that when the opposing party controls the House, there are generally more investigative hearing days targeting the executive branch compared to when the president's party controls the House.

required_elements: Accurately describes the inverse relationship — fewer hearing days under unified government
alternatives.correct_example: When the president's party controls the House, there are typically fewer investigative hearing days, while divided government produces more investigations of the executive branch.

wrong_examples: More investigative hearing days happen when the same party controls both — this is the opposite of what the data shows.
Describing only one president's situation without characterizing the overall relationship.
common_traps: The response must describe the pattern across the full graph, not just one data point
Accept responses that use the terms 'unified government' and 'divided government' as long as the relationship is accurately described
scoring_points.official_rubric: One point is earned for describing the relationship between investigative hearing days and party control of the House and the presidency. Acceptable response: When the opposing party controls the House, there are generally more investigative hearing days compared to when the president's own party controls the House.

sample_responses.response: When the opposing party controls the House, there are generally more investigative hearing days targeting the executive branch compared to when the president's party controls the House.
sample_responses.commentary: Accurately describes the directional pattern shown in the data — more hearing days under divided government, fewer under unified government. Earns the point.

sample_responses.response: More hearing days occur when the president's party controls the House.
sample_responses.commentary: Describes the opposite of what the data shows — the data clearly shows more hearing days when the opposing party controls the House. 0 pts.

parts.prompt: Draw a conclusion about how the difference in congressional hearing days between 1970–1980 and 1990–2000 may have affected presidential power during those periods.
parts.scoring_notes: The response must draw a conclusion about presidential power based on the hearing day data — not just describe the data. Accept any accurately reasoned conclusion.

scoring_points.description: Draw a conclusion about how the difference in congressional hearing days between 1970–1980 and 1990–2000 may have affected presidential power during those periods.

required_elements: Draws a specific conclusion about presidential power based on comparing hearing day data from both periods
alternatives.correct_example: The higher number of congressional hearing days between 1970 and 1980 likely constrained presidential power during that period by creating pressure on the executive branch and limiting the president's freedom of action. In contrast, the lower number of hearing days between 1990 and 2000 may have allowed presidents in that era to exercise more power with less congressional scrutiny.

wrong_examples: Simply stating there were more hearing days in 1970–1980 — that describes the data but does not draw a conclusion about presidential power.
Stating that Congress controlled the executive during one period without connecting to hearing day data.
common_traps: The response must go beyond describing the data to drawing a conclusion about presidential power
Accept either direction of the conclusion as long as it is logically connected to the hearing day difference
scoring_points.official_rubric: One point is earned for drawing a conclusion about how the difference in congressional hearing days between 1970–1980 and 1990–2000 may have affected presidential power. Acceptable response: The higher number of hearing days during 1970–1980 likely constrained presidential power, while fewer hearing days during 1990–2000 may have allowed presidents to act with less congressional scrutiny.

sample_responses.response: The higher number of congressional hearing days during 1970–1980 likely constrained presidential power during that period by creating public pressure and limiting executive freedom of action. In contrast, the lower number of hearing days in 1990–2000 may have allowed presidents more latitude to exercise power without as much congressional scrutiny.
sample_responses.commentary: Goes beyond describing the data to draw a reasoned conclusion about how the difference in hearing days affected presidential power in each period. Earns the point.

sample_responses.response: There were more hearing days in 1970–1980 than in 1990–2000.
sample_responses.commentary: Describes the data without drawing a conclusion about its effect on presidential power. 0 pts.

parts.prompt: Explain whether the data in the bar graph for the years in which the president's party controlled the House reflect Madison's ideas in The Federalist 51.
parts.scoring_notes: The response must engage with Madison's ideas in Federalist 51 about checks and balances and explain whether/how the data supports or contradicts those ideas. Accept either conclusion as long as it is supported with accurate reasoning.

scoring_points.description: Explain whether the data in the bar graph for the years in which the president's party controlled the House reflect Madison's ideas in The Federalist 51.

required_elements: Identifies a key idea from Federalist 51 and explains whether the data supports or contradicts it
alternatives.correct_example: Madison argued in Federalist 51 that each branch of government would use its powers to check the others — 'ambition must be made to counteract ambition.' The data does not fully reflect this principle because when the president's party controls the House, hearing days drop significantly, suggesting that partisan loyalty can override the institutional incentive to check executive power. Madison's design relied on institutional interests, not partisan ones.

required_elements: Identifies a key idea from Federalist 51 and explains whether the data supports or contradicts it
alternatives.correct_example: Federalist 51 envisions a Congress that actively checks the executive branch. The data for years when the president's party controlled the House shows much lower investigative activity, which partially contradicts Madison's expectations — suggesting that shared party loyalty reduces the legislature's willingness to check the executive, even when the institutional power exists to do so.

wrong_examples: Madison wanted strong government — this is too vague and does not address the specific checks and balances argument in Federalist 51.
Restating the data without engaging with Federalist 51's ideas.
common_traps: The response must reference a specific idea from Federalist 51 and connect it to the data — not just mention the document's name
Both 'supports' and 'contradicts' answers can earn the point if the reasoning is accurate
scoring_points.official_rubric: One point is earned for explaining whether the data for years in which the president's party controlled the House reflect Madison's ideas in The Federalist 51. Acceptable explanation: The Federalist 51 assumes that there will be checks and balances but does not take into consideration the role of political parties. Madison states that the legislative branch would serve as a check on the executive. But if one party controls both the House and the presidency, the interest of the House leadership in the welfare of the political party might be stronger than holding a president of their own party accountable. This would lead to fewer investigations of the executive branch, which would be contrary to the idea of checks in Federalist 51.

sample_responses.response: Federalist 51 assumes that there will be checks and balances but does not take into consideration the role of political parties. Madison said the legislative branch would check the executive, but when one party controls both the House and the presidency, partisan loyalty can be stronger than the institutional interest in holding the president accountable. The drop in hearing days under unified party control shows that partisanship weakens the checks Madison envisioned, which is contrary to the ideas in Federalist 51.
sample_responses.commentary: Identifies a specific idea from Federalist 51 (ambition counteracting ambition / legislative check on executive) and explains that the data partially contradicts Madison because parties override institutional incentives. Earns the point.

sample_responses.response: Federalist 51 explains that each branch needs the means to resist encroachment by the others. The data showing many fewer investigative hearings when the president's party controls the House partially supports Madison's framework — Congress still has the institutional power to investigate — but also shows its limits, since party loyalty reduces how often Congress actually uses that power to check the executive.
sample_responses.commentary: Engages a specific Federalist 51 idea (each branch resisting encroachment) and explains the data's mixed relationship to that idea. Either 'supports' or 'contradicts' earns the point as long as the reasoning references a specific Federalist 51 argument.

sample_responses.response: Madison wrote Federalist 51 a long time ago and the data shows that Congress does not always investigate the president, so the data does not reflect his ideas.
sample_responses.commentary: Does not identify any specific idea from Federalist 51 — just references the document by name. The explanation of how the data relates to Madison's argument is too vague to earn the point.
